explanation: The property requires that when the system is in the TRANSITION state, and both 'supported' and 'good' are true, the system must eventually transition to the NOMINAL state. In atomic propositions: 'state_is_TRANSITION' represents being in the TRANSITION state, 'supported' is the system health indicator, 'good' signifies good sensor data, and 'state_is_NOMINAL' means the system is in the NOMINAL state. Thus, the specification 'G((state_is_TRANSITION & supported & good) -> F state_is_NOMINAL)' ensures that, at all times, whenever the autopilot is in TRANSITION and both system support and sensor readings are good, then at some point after, the system will reach the NOMINAL state. This captures the natural language requirement as it forces the desired state change only under the specified conditions. Alternate properties might incorrectly allow transitions under different circumstances or fail to require the transition once the conditions are met, so this form is directly faithful to the input requirement.

The requirement specifies that the autopilot shall change states from TRANSITION to NOMINAL whenever two conditions are met: the system is supported and the sensor data is good. In terms of atomic propositions, 'state_is_TRANSITION' is true when the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state and 'state_is_NOMINAL' is true when it is in the NOMINAL state. The conditions 'supported' and 'good' must both be true to trigger the transition. To encode this in LTL, we ensure that whenever the autopilot is currently in the TRANSITION state and both 'supported' and 'good' are true, it must transition to the NOMINAL state in the next time step. The following LTL formula expresses this: G((state_is_TRANSITION & supported & good) -> X state_is_NOMINAL). This states globally, whenever the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state and the 'supported' and 'good' signals are true, in the next state, the autopilot must be in the NOMINAL state. This best captures both the causality and the precise timing implied by the specification. Previous LTL formulas that might allow the transition to happen at any time in the future (using F or U operators) would not enforce the immediate transition, and would not strictly capture the 'shall change states when...' requirement.

The property states that whenever the autopilot is currently in the TRANSITION state, and both 'supported' and 'good' are true (which together indicate 'the system is supported and sensor data is good'), then eventually (i.e., as soon as those conditions are met) the autopilot will change its state to NOMINAL. The LTL formula expresses: globally (at all times), if the autopilot is in TRANSITION and system is supported and sensors are good, then eventually it will be in NOMINAL. This captures the requirement that the state change must happen when the specified conditions hold. This encoding does not use numerical comparisons, as required, and uses only atomic propositions. Previous LTL encodings may have missed the requirement to only change state when BOTH conditions are true, or may not have explicitly captured the cause-effect relation between the states as described here.

The input states that the transition from TRANSITION to NOMINAL should only occur when both 'supported' and 'good' are true (i.e., the system is supported and the sensor data is good). In atomic propositions, 'state_is_TRANSITION' is true when the vehicle is in the TRANSITION state, and 'state_is_NOMINAL' is true when it is in the NOMINAL state. To capture the requirement, we state that whenever the system is in the TRANSITION state and transitions to the NOMINAL state in the next step, it must be the case that both 'supported' and 'good' are true in the current state (i.e., the transition is only possible when both conditions are met). This can be formalized as: Globally, if in the current step the autopilot is in TRANSITION and in the next step it will be in NOMINAL, then currently both 'supported' and 'good' must hold. Previous LTLs might either not check for the simultaneous status of 'supported' and 'good' at the moment of transition, or might not restrict all transitions from TRANSITION to NOMINAL, but this LTL directly enforces the requirement for every such transition.

The requirement specifies that the autopilot must change from the TRANSITION state to the NOMINAL state when both 'supported' and 'good' are true. In LTL, this is best represented by stating: Whenever the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state, and at some point both 'supported' and 'good' are true, it must eventually (but as soon as both are true) enter the NOMINAL state. This can be formalized as: whenever we have (state_is_TRANSITION & supported & good), then eventually (state_is_NOMINAL) must happen while remaining in TRANSITION until that point. This is captured by G((state_is_TRANSITION & supported & good) -> (state_is_TRANSITION U state_is_NOMINAL)). This ensures that change only happens if the conditions are met, and as soon as they are, NOMINAL is entered.

The requirement states that the autopilot should change states from TRANSITION to NOMINAL when two conditions are met: (1) the system is supported and (2) the sensor data is good. In atomic propositions, 'state_is_TRANSITION' and 'state_is_NOMINAL' indicate the current state. 'supported' and 'good' are indicators for the required conditions. The LTL formula says: globally (always), if the system is in the TRANSITION state, and both 'supported' and 'good' are true, then eventually (at some point in the future), the state becomes NOMINAL. This is captured by: G((state_is_TRANSITION & supported & good) -> F state_is_NOMINAL). It specifically enforces that the transition to NOMINAL must eventually occur whenever the conditions are met. Previous formulations that didn't specify both 'supported' and 'good' or didn't relate the TRANSITION and NOMINAL states directly would not capture all aspects of the requirement, such as incorrectly allowing the transition without both preconditions satisfied.

The requirement states that the autopilot should only transition from TRANSITION to NOMINAL if both the system is 'supported' and sensor data is 'good'. In terms of atomic propositions, we want to ensure that whenever the state is TRANSITION, if in the next step the state becomes NOMINAL, then at the current step both 'supported' and 'good' are true. This is expressed using the following LTL formula:

G((state_is_TRANSITION & X state_is_NOMINAL) -> (supported & good))

This ensures that a transition from TRANSITION to NOMINAL only happens when the specified conditions ('supported' and 'good') are true. Other translation approaches might miss the requirement that these conditions are strictly needed at the point of transition, or might incorrectly require them for the entire duration of TRANSITION or NOMINAL states, which would be less precise and not capture the 'when the system is supported and sensor data is good' part exactly when the state change occurs.

The requirement specifies that the autopilot should transition from the TRANSITION state to the NOMINAL state, but only if both 'supported' is true and 'good' (sensor data) is true. This is a causality property: being in the TRANSITION state and having both system support and good sensor data should eventually result in the NOMINAL state. The LTL formula expresses that whenever the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state and both 'supported' and 'good' are true, then it will eventually reach the NOMINAL state (without leaving TRANSITION until either supported/good is true and switching to NOMINAL, or staying in TRANSITION until conditions allow). We use G (globally) for the always condition: whenever (state_is_TRANSITION & supported & good) happens, there is an eventual transition to state_is_NOMINAL, while optionally using U to specify that this is the first next state. No LTL property previously provided, but common pitfalls would include not conditioning the transition on supported & good, or not specifying the direction of state change. This translation covers the intent because it ties the transition event directly to the conjunction of the two conditions.

The natural language input specifies that a change of states from TRANSITION to NOMINAL should only happen when 'supported' and 'good' are both true. In terms of atomic propositions, this would be a property about transitions: specifically, that whenever the autopilot leaves the TRANSITION state and enters the NOMINAL state, it must be the case that both 'supported' and 'good' are true at that moment. To express this in LTL, we check every point where a transition from 'state_is_TRANSITION' to 'state_is_NOMINAL' occurs (i.e., 'state_is_TRANSITION' is true in the previous step, and 'state_is_NOMINAL' is true in the current step), and require that 'supported' and 'good' are both true when the transition occurs. The formula uses the 'G' (globally) operator to say this holds at all times, the 'X' (next) operator to check the next step, and standard boolean logic.

The property requires that if the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state, and both 'supported' and 'good' are true (i.e., the system is supported and sensor data is good), then eventually the autopilot shall enter the NOMINAL state. This is best captured in LTL as: globally, if the current state is TRANSITION and both supported and good hold, then eventually the state becomes NOMINAL. The given LTL directly encodes the condition and required state transition without using any numerical comparisons or unsupported semantics.
